summary: Fournier reflects on how her natural tendency toward enforcing rules and holding people accountable, which worked well as a peer, has become problematic as she's moved up the management chain. She identifies three key issues: it causes team members to stop holding each other accountable, it's demoralizing when correction comes from a senior leader, and she's now too far removed from day-to-day work to know what rules make sense. She concludes that her role should shift from rule-making and enforcement to setting high-level goals and empowering teams to create their own practices.
key_points: When a boss enforces accountability, team members stop holding each other accountable because they view it as 'the boss's job'
Corrective feedback from senior leaders is amplified and more demoralizing than the same feedback from peers
Leaders become too removed from daily work to know which rules and practices actually make sense for the current team
High-performing teams hold each other accountable; feedback should come quickly and naturally from peers
The leader's job is to set high-level goals and let teams iterate on processes until they find what works for them
money_quotes: A not-so-secret aspect of my personality is that I have no problem with the policing aspects of leadership.
It seems that having me hold individuals accountable ends up causing some individuals to stop holding each other accountable.
It is still hard to get used to the idea that I am not just one of the team, and that everything I do is amplified and taken in ways I don't always anticipate.
It is important that I'm seen as an inspirational figure to my team, someone they look up to and look forward to interacting with, and not vice-versa.
The only true best practice out there is to let your team take a concept or process and iterate on it until it takes on a form that works effectively for them.
I'm getting out of the business of being the rule maker and rule enforcer.
I want to work myself out of a job, after all, and the best ideas don't come from me, so why should the policing?
key_insight: As leaders rise in seniority, their role must shift from enforcing accountability to creating conditions where teams hold themselves accountable.
